TSMC is accelerating its most advanced US plant in Arizona (for 2nm & A16 chips) while slowing plans in Japan & Europe.

This isn't just a business decision; it reveals a two-speed semiconductor market: explosive demand for AI chips vs. soft demand for everything else. (1/2)

The world's most advanced chips are made by foundries. The best is TSMC in Taiwan. 

Due to US export controls, TSMC can't make cutting-edge chips for Huawei. This forces China to rely on its domestic foundry, SMIC, which is years behind in tech. /3

Micron's plan isn't just to make HBM in the US, but to also build the advanced packaging capabilities here. This is a direct attempt to de-risk the AI supply chain from its heavy reliance on a single company (TSMC) in a single region (Taiwan). /4

But the REAL chokepoint is advanced packaging. Think of it as the hyper-complex, microscopic plumbing that connects the HBM to the GPU. This is where TSMC has dominated with CoWoS. The world's capacity for this is extremely limited and concentrated in Taiwan. /3

Inertial Analogy. 

Think of a large particle as an oil tanker, trying to navigate a very, tight channel while passing thru a canal.

The channel buoys are the charged fibers.

Too heavy, and slow to react, the tanker contacts a buoy and gets trapped. That's particles greater than 1 micron.

4/
Particle filtering mechanisms

Inertial Impaction & Interception.
Typically affecting larger particles greater than 1 micron, or 1000 nm.

These larger particles can't follow the tight turning air streams around filter fibers, and end up contacting fiber and sticking.
